questions.question: Explain the concepts of abstraction and encapsulation in Python Object Oriented Programming. With the help of a comprehensive example, demonstrate how @property decorators are used to implement controlled access to private attributes, showing getter, setter, and deleter methods. Also explain how Python's convention-based privacy mechanism works with single underscore (_) and double underscore (__) prefixes, including the concept of name mangling.
keyPoints: Abstraction: hiding complex implementation details, showing only essential features of an object
Encapsulation: bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class)
Python's convention-based privacy: single underscore (_) indicates protected, double underscore (__) triggers name mangling
@property decorator converts methods to behave like attributes
Getter: @property, Setter: @method.setter, Deleter: @method.deleter
Name mangling: __attr becomes _ClassName__attr internally
Controlled validation in setters prevents invalid state

questions.question: A banking application requires a 'BankAccount' class that encapsulates account holder information, balance, and transaction history. Using Python's data classes (@dataclass), design a robust solution that: (a) Uses proper encapsulation to protect sensitive account data, (b) Implements methods for deposit and withdrawal with proper validation, (c) Maintains an immutable account number, (d) Provides a string representation for debugging. Write complete Python code demonstrating these concepts and explain the advantages of using @dataclass for such scenarios.
keyPoints: @dataclass automatically generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__ methods
Use field() with default_factory for mutable default values like lists
Use frozen=True for immutability where needed
BankAccount fields: account_number (immutable), holder_name, balance, transactions
Validation in deposit/withdrawal methods (negative amounts, insufficient funds)
Encapsulation through private fields (underscore prefix) with property decorators
Advantages: reduced boilerplate code, type hints support, automatic comparisons

questions.question: Design a 'Student' class for a university management system that demonstrates abstraction by providing a clean public interface while hiding internal implementation details. The class should: (a) Store student ID, name, grades for multiple subjects, and CGPA, (b) Use private attributes with proper encapsulation, (c) Calculate CGPA automatically when grades are updated, (d) Provide methods to add/remove courses and update grades. Implement this using @property decorators and explain how abstraction simplifies the interface for end-users of this class.
keyPoints: Abstraction: external users interact with simple methods, internal complexity hidden
Private attributes: _name, _student_id, _grades, _cgpa
@property for controlled access to private attributes
Setter methods validate input before modifying internal state
CGPA calculation encapsulated as internal logic, not exposed to users
Clean public interface: add_course(), update_grade(), get_cgpa()
Internal implementation details (calculation logic) hidden from users

questions.question: What is name mangling in Python? Explain with a detailed example how double underscore (__) prefix works differently from single underscore (_) prefix. Create a class hierarchy (parent and child class) to demonstrate how name mangling affects attribute access in inheritance scenarios. Also explain when and why you would use each type of access modifier in practical Python programming.
keyPoints: Name mangling: Python transforms __attr to _ClassName__attr at compile time
Single underscore (_): convention only, no actual restriction, signals 'internal use'
Double underscore (__): triggers name mangling to prevent accidental access/override
Inheritance example showing how child class cannot directly access parent.__private
Access through mangled name: _ParentClass__attr
Practical use: _ for internal helpers, __ for true privacy within class hierarchy
Python philosophy: 'we are all consenting adults' - convention over enforcement

questions.question: Compare traditional classes with @dataclass in Python. Create a scenario where using @dataclass significantly reduces code complexity while maintaining proper encapsulation. Write Python code demonstrating a 'Product' inventory class using @dataclass with proper validation, and explain how data classes support the principles of encapsulation and abstraction.
keyPoints: Traditional classes require manual __init__, __repr__, __eq__ implementations
@dataclass generates these automatically, reducing boilerplate
Data classes support field() for custom default values and validation
Use __post_init__ for validation after initialization
Product example: product_id, name, price, quantity, category
Validation in __post_init__ ensures price and quantity are positive
Encapsulation: private fields with __ (frozen option for immutability)
Abstraction: clean interface, internal validation logic hidden
